lines.text: APPENDIX
Key dates
1985: Sinsheimer hosts the Santa Cruz meeting; Dulbecco's editorial in Science.
1987: DeLisi at DOE begins formal planning.
October 1, 1990: Human Genome Project officially launches, jointly led by NIH and DOE.
1992: James Watson resigns as NHGRI director; Sanger Centre founded at Hinxton.
1993: Francis Collins becomes NHGRI director.
1995: TIGR publishes the complete genome of Haemophilus influenzae — first complete genome of a free-living organism.
February 1996: Bermuda Principles adopted — public data release within 24 hours.
May 1998: Craig Venter announces Celera Genomics.
June 26, 2000: White House announcement of the draft genome by Clinton, Blair, Collins, and Venter.
February 2001: Public consortium publishes draft in Nature; Celera publishes draft in Science.
April 14, 2003: Finished sequence announced — 50th anniversary of Watson-Crick.
2003: ENCODE launched; HapMap data collection underway.
2006: TCGA launched; UK Biobank launched.
2008: GINA signed into law; 1000 Genomes Project launched.
2012: ENCODE 3 main publications.
2015: 1000 Genomes Project completed.
April 2022: T2T-CHM13 published — first truly complete human genome.
May 2023: Human Pangenome Reference Consortium publishes first draft pangenome.
2026 (today): Whole genome sequencing for under $200 in a day.
Key institutions
NHGRI — National Human Genome Research Institute, NIH; project coordinator for the U.
S.
DOE — U.
S.
Department of Energy; co-led the U.
S. effort.
Wellcome Sanger Institute — Hinxton, UK; largest single sequencing contributor.
Whitehead/MIT — Eric Lander's center.
Washington University in St.
Louis — Robert Waterston's center.
Baylor College of Medicine — Richard Gibbs's center.
Genoscope — French national sequencing center.
RIKEN — Japanese genomics contributor.
BGI — Beijing Genomics Institute; late entrant, now major global player.
Celera Genomics — Venter's private competitor.
TIGR — The Institute for Genomic Research; Venter's nonprofit, 1995 H. influenzae.
Key concepts to retain
Sanger sequencing: the chain-termination method used throughout HGP; ~500-900 bases per read.
BAC: Bacterial Artificial Chromosome; ~150 kb vector used in clone-by-clone mapping.
Clone-by-clone vs whole-genome shotgun: the two competing strategies; both turned out to be necessary.
Bermuda Principles: 24-hour public data release; arguably the most consequential single policy decision.
Euchromatin vs heterochromatin: ~92% vs ~8% of the genome; the 2003 finished sequence covered euchromatin only.
Polygenic: hundreds or thousands of small-effect variants influencing a trait — the dominant pattern for common disease.
Missing heritability: the gap between twin-study heritability and GWAS-explained heritability.
ELSI: the 5% set-aside for Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications research.
T2T: Telomere-to-Telomere consortium; 2022 completion using long reads.
Pangenome: graph-based reference replacing the single linear reference.
Suggested further reading
John Sulston and Georgina Ferry, The Common Thread (2002) — Sulston's memoir of the public effort.
James Shreeve, The Genome War (2004) — narrative history of the public-vs-Celera race.
Walter Bodmer and Robin McKie, The Book of Man — broader genetics context.
Lander et al., Nature 2001 — the public consortium draft paper.
Venter et al., Science 2001 — the Celera draft paper.
International Human Genome Sequencing Consortium, Nature 2004 — the finished sequence paper.
Nurk et al., Science 2022 — T2T-CHM13 publication.
Liao et al., Nature 2023 — Human Pangenome Reference Consortium draft.
